meanings.definition: To leave a place suddenly or secretly.
examples: The thieves decamped before the police arrived.
He decamped with all the money in the middle of the night.

meanings.definition: Feeding on the flesh of other animals.
examples: Lions are carnivorous animals that hunt for their food.
The carnivorous plant trapped insects in its leaves.

meanings.definition: An aromatic gum resin that releases fragrant fumes when burned.
examples: The temple was filled with the scent of burning frankincense.
Frankincense and myrrh were among the gifts brought to the child.

meanings.definition: A person appointed to act for another in managing property, rights, or interests.
examples: The assignee was authorized to collect the debts.
As assignee, she took over the lease from the original tenant.

meanings.definition: A town, city, or district that has its own local government.
examples: The municipality is responsible for collecting the garbage.
Each municipality sets its own property tax rates.

meanings.definition: Something that has actual, real existence as opposed to opinion or interpretation.
examples: As a matter of fact, the meeting was rescheduled to Friday.
Whether he was present is a matter of fact for the jury to decide.

meanings.definition: To stretch or reach out too far, often beyond one's ability or limits.
examples: He overreached and grabbed at a branch that was too far away.
The company overreached by expanding into too many markets at once.

meanings.definition: Knowing or using several languages.
examples: The polyglot diplomat negotiated in four different tongues.
She grew up in a polyglot city where many languages were spoken.

meanings.definition: A standard, model, or pattern regarded as typical or expected.
examples: Working from home has become the norm for many employees.
Her grades were well above the norm for her class.

meanings.definition: A person authorized to act or vote on behalf of another.
examples: She voted by proxy because she could not attend the meeting.
He appointed his brother as his proxy at the shareholders' vote.

meanings.definition: Something that serves as a substitute or stand-in for something else.
examples: Test scores are often used as a proxy for academic ability.

meanings.definition: A dramatic work with sensational events and exaggerated emotions intended to appeal strongly to the feelings.
examples: The play was a melodrama full of villains and tearful heroines.
The TV series descended into pure melodrama.

meanings.definition: Exaggerated and emotional behavior or a situation full of overblown drama.
examples: There was a lot of melodrama over who would sit where.

meanings.definition: Solid matter that settles to the bottom of a liquid.
examples: A layer of sediment collected at the bottom of the wine bottle.
The river deposited sediment along its banks.

meanings.definition: A feeling of keen annoyance or distress caused by failure or disappointment.
examples: To his chagrin, he had forgotten his lines on stage.
She felt a deep chagrin at having lost the contest.

meanings.definition: To give a false or misleading account of something.
examples: The article misrepresented her views entirely.
He was accused of misrepresenting the product's benefits.

meanings.definition: A large, important, or chief city of a country or region.
examples: New York is a bustling metropolis.
She moved from a small town to the metropolis for work.

meanings.definition: To a great or extreme degree; harshly or seriously.
examples: He was severely injured in the accident.
The region was severely affected by the drought.

meanings.definition: Easily seen, noticed, or perceived.
examples: There was a noticeable improvement in her work.
He had a noticeable limp after the injury.

meanings.definition: The highest point of success, power, or greatness.
examples: The empire reached its zenith in the second century.
She was at the zenith of her career when she retired.

meanings.definition: The point in the sky directly overhead.
examples: At noon the sun was nearly at its zenith.

meanings.definition: Genuine, excellent, and of high quality.
examples: She did a sterling job organizing the event.
He has a sterling reputation for honesty.

meanings.definition: Incapable of being stretched or extended; of fixed length or area.
examples: The cable is made of an inextensible material that will not stretch.
The rope proved inextensible no matter how hard they pulled.

meanings.definition: Light, teasing, and good-natured banter.
examples: The dinner party was full of witty persiflage.
Their conversation was mostly playful persiflage.

meanings.definition: A short, pointed statement expressing a general truth or principle.
examples: 'Less is more' is a well-known aphorism.
The book is full of clever aphorisms about life.

meanings.definition: Conspicuously and openly offensive or scandalous.
examples: The referee called a flagrant foul.
It was a flagrant violation of the rules.

meanings.definition: Making a particular outcome likely or helping to bring it about.
examples: A quiet room is conducive to study.
The warm climate is conducive to growing citrus fruit.

meanings.definition: To join together or unite.
examples: The two streams conjoin to form a wider river.
They conjoined their efforts to finish the project.

meanings.definition: A person who explores or searches an area, especially for minerals.
examples: The prospector panned for gold in the river.
An old prospector discovered the silver vein.

meanings.definition: Moving easily and gracefully; nimble and supple.
examples: The dancer's lithesome movements captivated the audience.
She had a lithesome figure that suited ballet.

meanings.definition: A messenger sent out to spread a teaching, especially one acting under divine authority.
examples: The apostle traveled across the empire to preach.
He became an apostle of the new political movement.

meanings.definition: An enthusiastic supporter or promoter of a cause or idea.
examples: She was an apostle of free education for all.

meanings.definition: An expression of great enthusiasm, joy, or rapture.
examples: He went into rhapsodies over the delicious meal.
Her letter was a rhapsody of praise for the new opera.

meanings.definition: A free-flowing, emotionally expressive piece of music.
examples: The pianist performed a Hungarian rhapsody.

meanings.definition: An expression of sympathy for someone who is suffering grief or misfortune.
examples: She sent her condolences to the grieving family.
He offered his condolences after hearing of their loss.

meanings.definition: One of several different arrangements or orderings of the same set of items.
examples: There are six permutations of the letters A, B, and C.
The team tried every permutation of players to find the best lineup.

meanings.definition: A serious crime, typically punishable by imprisonment for more than a year or by death.
examples: He was convicted of a felony and sentenced to prison.
Armed robbery is classified as a felony.

meanings.definition: To make familiar with something through habit or repeated use.
examples: It took her a while to accustom herself to the cold climate.
You must accustom your eyes to the dark.

meanings.definition: Something assumed to be true without proof; a guess or conjecture.
examples: His theory rests on a single unproven supposition.
On the supposition that it will rain, bring an umbrella.

meanings.definition: To leave property or money to someone by means of a will.
examples: She bequeathed her estate to her grandchildren.
He bequeathed his books to the local library.

meanings.definition: To pass something on to those who come later.
examples: Their generation bequeathed a legacy of scientific progress.

meanings.definition: Suggesting or expressing that someone has done something wrong.
examples: She gave him an accusatory look.
His accusatory tone made everyone defensive.

meanings.definition: To make dough rise and become light through fermentation, as with yeast.
examples: Bakers leaven bread with yeast.
The dough was leavened overnight before baking.

meanings.definition: To lighten or enliven something, especially with a different quality.
examples: She leavened her serious speech with a few jokes.

meanings.definition: To form ideas or explanations through speculation rather than proof.
examples: Scientists theorize that the planet once had liquid water.
He theorized about the causes of the economic collapse.

meanings.definition: The boundary line or distance around a circle or other round shape.
examples: The circumference of the wheel is about three feet.
They measured the circumference of the tree trunk.

meanings.definition: The widespread and brutal killing of many people.
examples: The battle left terrible carnage on the field.
Rescuers were horrified by the carnage at the crash site.

meanings.definition: Having the sails set for sailing as close to the direction of the wind as possible.
examples: The yacht was close-hauled as it beat against the wind.
Sailing close-hauled, they tacked toward the harbor.

meanings.definition: The ability to read and write.
examples: The program aims to improve adult literacy.
Literacy rates have risen sharply in the region.

meanings.definition: Competence or knowledge in a specified area.
examples: Digital literacy is essential in today's workplace.

meanings.definition: Being the last or final, beyond which there is nothing else.
examples: Death is the ultimate fate of all living things.
Their ultimate goal was complete independence.

meanings.definition: Representing the greatest or most extreme example of something.
examples: This sports car is the ultimate driving machine.

meanings.definition: A person who likes or admires women.
examples: He was known as a philogynist who championed women's rights.
Unlike his cynical friend, he was a true philogynist.

meanings.definition: Following one after another in order of time or sequence.
examples: The sequent chapters build on one another.
Each sequent event led inevitably to the next.

meanings.definition: To take a dispute to a court of law to be resolved.
examples: The two companies chose to litigate the contract dispute.
They were prepared to litigate rather than settle.

meanings.definition: To be exceptionally good at or superior in something.
examples: She excels at mathematics.
He excelled in every sport he tried.

meanings.definition: A written law passed by a legislative body or authority.
examples: The new statute bans smoking in public buildings.
Such conduct is prohibited by statute.

meanings.definition: The study and collection of postage stamps.
examples: His interest in philately began as a child.
Philately taught her a great deal about world history.

meanings.definition: To happen at the same time or to correspond exactly.
examples: My vacation coincides with the holidays this year.
Their views on the matter coincided perfectly.

meanings.definition: Aware of and responsive to one's own existence, feelings, and surroundings.
examples: She was still conscious after the fall and able to answer the paramedics' questions.
Being a conscious, thinking being sets humans apart in many ways.

meanings.definition: Deliberately done while fully aware of what one is doing.
examples: He made a conscious effort to speak more slowly during the presentation.

meanings.definition: Arising naturally from within, without any external cause or planning.
examples: Her spontaneous laughter filled the room.
The crowd broke into spontaneous applause.

meanings.definition: An act of mixing things or changing their order.
examples: After a quick shuffle of the cards, the dealer began the game.
A shuffle of the schedule moved the meeting to Friday.

meanings.definition: To walk by dragging the feet slowly along the ground.
examples: The tired children began to shuffle toward their beds.

meanings.definition: A person or company that sells goods or services.
examples: A street vendor sold roasted nuts on the corner.
The company chose a new software vendor for its accounting needs.

meanings.definition: The use of words to convey the opposite of their literal meaning, often as veiled mockery.
examples: "What lovely weather," she said with heavy irony as rain poured down.

meanings.definition: A situation whose outcome is strangely contrary to what one would expect.
examples: The irony was that the fire station itself burned down.

meanings.definition: Innocent and honest, with no trace of deceit or cunning.
examples: Her guileless answer made it clear she had nothing to hide.
The guileless child believed every story the older boys told him.

meanings.definition: Suggesting or threatening evil, harm, or misfortune.
examples: A sinister figure lurked in the shadows of the alley.
There was a sinister tone in his quiet warning.

meanings.definition: The gradual wearing away or destruction of a material, especially metal, by chemical action.
examples: Years of salt spray caused severe corrosion on the bridge's beams.
Rust is a common form of corrosion on iron.

meanings.definition: Unable to exist or work together in harmony.
examples: The old printer is incompatible with the new computer.
They divorced because their goals proved completely incompatible.

meanings.definition: Attractive in a showy or vulgar way but actually false or worthless.
examples: The film's meretricious special effects could not hide its empty plot.
Critics dismissed the jewelry as meretricious glitter with no real value.

meanings.definition: The science or study of correct reasoning.
examples: A course in logic teaches students to spot flaws in an argument.

meanings.definition: A particular way of reasoning or the soundness of it.
examples: I couldn't follow the logic behind his decision.

meanings.definition: A state of heavy, oppressive drowsiness or sleepiness.
examples: The warm afternoon brought on a deep somnolence she could barely shake.
A wave of somnolence overtook the audience during the long lecture.

meanings.definition: A long, narrow crack or opening in a surface.
examples: Water seeped through a fissure in the rock.
A deep fissure ran across the dried-up riverbed.

meanings.definition: Looking back on or dealing with past events or situations.
examples: The article offered a retrospective view of the decade's music.
In a retrospective mood, she leafed through old photographs.

meanings.definition: An exhibition or compilation showing the development of an artist's work over time.
examples: The museum held a retrospective of the painter's entire career.

meanings.definition: A disappointing decline after a build-up of importance or excitement.
examples: After months of hype, the finale was a complete anticlimax.
Winning by default felt like an anticlimax to the eager team.

meanings.definition: To severely damage or disfigure, often by removing a part.
examples: Vandals mutilated the statue by breaking off its arms.
The disease can mutilate tissue if left untreated.

meanings.definition: To annoy, irritate, or wear out.
examples: It irks me when people leave the lights on all night.
Her constant tardiness began to irk the whole team.

meanings.definition: A person who exploits circumstances to gain an advantage, often regardless of principles.
examples: The opportunist bought up land cheaply right after the disaster.
Critics called him a political opportunist who switched sides for votes.

meanings.definition: A small, rare, or unusual object valued as a curiosity.
examples: She kept a shelf of curios collected from her travels.
The shop was crowded with curios from distant countries.

meanings.definition: Lack of reverence toward God or sacred things.
examples: The villagers viewed his mocking remarks as an act of impiety.
She was accused of impiety for disrupting the religious service.

meanings.definition: The branch of botany dealing with the study of trees and shrubs.
examples: Her work in dendrology focused on the oak species of North America.
A course in dendrology teaches students to identify trees by their bark and leaves.

meanings.definition: Deep sorrow, grief, or lamentation.
examples: The poem was filled with the dolor of lost love.
Her voice carried a dolor that silenced the room.

meanings.definition: A deceptive movement or pretense intended to mislead an opponent.
examples: The boxer threw a feint with his left before landing a right hook.
Her offer of help was only a feint to distract him.

meanings.definition: The act of demanding something firmly and persistently.
examples: At his insistence, we postponed the trip until spring.
Her insistence on accuracy made the report far more reliable.

meanings.definition: A favorable sign or protective influence; (usually plural) support or sponsorship.
examples: The festival was held under the auspices of the city council.
They set out on the journey under happy auspices.

meanings.definition: Habitually truthful and honest.
examples: A veracious witness, she never embellished her account.
His veracious reporting earned the trust of his readers.

meanings.definition: A book size made by folding each sheet of paper into eight leaves.
examples: The library owned a rare octavo printed in the seventeenth century.
The poems were published in a slim octavo volume.

meanings.definition: Expressed clearly in few words; concise.
examples: Her succinct summary captured the whole argument in two sentences.
He gave a succinct reply and moved on to the next question.

meanings.definition: A claim or assertion of a right or quality, often without real justification.
examples: He had no pretension to being an expert in the field.
Her pretension of wealth fooled no one for long.

meanings.definition: Allowed or permitted, especially by rules or law.
examples: Quiet conversation is permissible in the library's lounge.
It is not permissible to park here during business hours.

meanings.definition: In a confused, agitated state of mind, often from fever or illness.
examples: The patient was delirious with fever and didn't recognize his family.

meanings.definition: Wildly excited or ecstatic.
examples: The fans were delirious when their team scored the winning goal.

meanings.definition: An enthusiastic expression of approval or applause.
examples: The young scientist's discovery earned plaudits from her colleagues.
His performance drew loud plaudits from the audience.

meanings.definition: Able to be passed or sent from one person, place, or thing to another.
examples: The disease is transmissible through close contact.
The signal is transmissible over long distances by satellite.

meanings.definition: A flat, revolving circular stone used for sharpening tools or grinding things.
examples: He sharpened the axe blade on the old grindstone.
Sparks flew as the knife met the spinning grindstone.

meanings.definition: The mingling or blending of the worship of different gods, as in polytheism.
examples: Ancient empires often practiced theocrasy, merging local gods into one cult.
The temple's rituals reflected a theocrasy drawn from several traditions.

meanings.definition: Concerned with knowledge or ideas for their own sake rather than practical use.
examples: Her research is purely theoretical and has no immediate application.
The course covers the theoretical foundations of economics.

meanings.definition: Showing the mental or physical weakness associated with old age.
examples: His senile forgetfulness made it hard for him to live alone.
The senile patient often confused the past with the present.

meanings.definition: To gradually become smaller, fewer, or weaker.
examples: Our supplies began to dwindle as the storm wore on.
The crowd dwindled to a handful by the end of the night.

meanings.definition: The act of examining two or more things to note their similarities and differences.
examples: A comparison of the two phones showed the newer model was faster.
By comparison, last year's harvest was much larger.

meanings.definition: To change from a liquid into a thickened, clotted, or solid mass.
examples: Blood will coagulate quickly to seal a wound.
The heat caused the egg whites to coagulate.

meanings.definition: The state of having, owning, or controlling something.
examples: The police found stolen goods in his possession.

meanings.definition: A thing that is owned.
examples: She packed her few possessions into a single suitcase.

meanings.definition: Descended from the same ancestor; related by blood.
examples: The law forbids marriage between consanguineous relatives.
The two families were consanguineous, sharing a common great-grandfather.

meanings.definition: A long, formal written work that examines a subject in detail.
examples: He wrote a lengthy treatise on the history of economics.
Her treatise on ethics is still studied in universities today.

meanings.definition: Producing a good or helpful result; advantageous.
examples: Regular exercise is beneficial to your health.
The new policy proved beneficial for small businesses.

meanings.definition: To leave someone abandoned in a remote or inaccessible place.
examples: The mutineers marooned their captain on a deserted island.
Stranded by the flood, the hikers were marooned for two days.

meanings.definition: To twice the degree or extent; in two ways.
examples: Be doubly careful when crossing the icy road.
She was doubly pleased by the praise and the promotion.

meanings.definition: Full of or expressing sorrow; melancholy.
examples: A dolesome wind moaned through the empty house.
His dolesome expression told us the news was bad.

meanings.definition: Fully prepared for a particular purpose or action.
examples: Dinner is ready, so please come to the table.
Are you ready to leave for the airport?

meanings.definition: Quick or prompt to act or respond.
examples: She always has a ready answer for every question.

meanings.definition: Too small or unimportant to be worth notice.
examples: The cost was insignificant compared to the benefits.
He felt insignificant standing before the vast mountain range.

meanings.definition: The keeping or celebrating of a custom, rule, or ceremony.
examples: The observance of the holiday includes a parade and fireworks.
Strict observance of the rules is expected of all members.

meanings.definition: Strictness, severity, or inflexibility in standards or conditions.
examples: The scientist tested her theory with great rigor.
The recruits endured the rigor of basic training.

meanings.definition: A scraped or worn area, or the material rubbed off by friction.
examples: The fall left a painful abrasion on his knee.
Constant abrasion had worn the stone steps smooth.

meanings.definition: To leave or give up occupancy of a place.
examples: Guests must vacate their rooms by noon.
The tenants were ordered to vacate the building.

meanings.definition: To officially cancel or annul, as a legal ruling.
examples: The judge agreed to vacate the earlier verdict.

meanings.definition: The act or sense of hearing.
examples: The test measured the patient's audition across different pitches.
Audition is one of the five basic senses.

meanings.definition: A trial performance to test a performer's suitability.
examples: She landed the lead role after a nerve-wracking audition.

meanings.definition: Guided by or showing careful, sensible judgment.
examples: For prudential reasons, they kept some savings in reserve.
The board made a prudential decision to delay the expansion.

meanings.definition: Eager or inclined to fight; warlike.
examples: The bellicose general pushed for an immediate invasion.
His bellicose remarks raised tensions between the two nations.

meanings.definition: Relating to or providing instruction in many technical or applied arts and sciences.
examples: She enrolled at a polytechnic institute to study engineering.
The polytechnic curriculum covered everything from mechanics to design.

meanings.definition: So strong or well-defended that it cannot be captured or broken into.
examples: The fortress stood impregnable atop the cliff.
Their lead seemed impregnable until the final minute of the game.

meanings.definition: The state of being important, well-known, or easily noticed.
examples: The scandal brought the senator unwanted prominence.
Her research rose to prominence in the scientific community.

meanings.definition: Showing good sense and careful judgment.
examples: A judicious use of spices made the dish delicious.
She was judicious in choosing which battles to fight.

meanings.definition: Causing fear, horror, or alarm.
examples: A frightful scream echoed through the dark forest.
The storm did frightful damage to the coastal town.

meanings.definition: A hammer with a large head, usually made of wood.
examples: He tapped the tent pegs into the ground with a mallet.
The sculptor used a mallet and chisel to shape the stone.

meanings.definition: Done or achieved easily, often without much thought or effort.
examples: He gave a facile answer that ignored the real problem.
Their facile victory left the team overconfident.

meanings.definition: The practice or profession of examining the eyes and measuring vision.
examples: She studied optometry to become an eye-care specialist.
A degree in optometry qualifies you to prescribe corrective lenses.

meanings.definition: To become progressively worse in quality or condition.
examples: His health began to deteriorate after the long illness.
The old bridge has deteriorated badly over the years.

meanings.definition: To confuse or puzzle someone completely.
examples: The riddle baffled even the cleverest students.
Scientists were baffled by the strange readings.

meanings.definition: Lacking skill or competence; clumsy.
examples: His inept handling of the crisis made things worse.
The inept waiter spilled soup on two customers.

meanings.definition: Impressive grandeur, splendor, or greatness.
examples: Visitors marveled at the magnificence of the cathedral.
The magnificence of the sunset left them speechless.

meanings.definition: An earlier event or action regarded as a guide or example for later ones.
examples: The ruling set a precedent for future court cases.
There was no precedent for such an unusual request.

meanings.definition: A countless or extremely large number of things.
examples: A myriad of stars filled the night sky.
She faced a myriad of choices when planning the trip.

meanings.definition: Countless or extremely numerous.
examples: The project failed for myriad reasons.

meanings.definition: A vast cloud of gas and dust in interstellar space, often glowing or appearing as a hazy patch of light.
examples: Through the telescope, the nebula glowed in soft shades of pink and blue.
Stars are born within the dense regions of a nebula.

meanings.definition: Extending or traveling across an entire continent.
examples: The transcontinental railroad linked the east and west coasts of the country.
They took a transcontinental flight from New York to Los Angeles.

meanings.definition: The quality of being final, settled, and beyond change.
examples: The judge spoke with a finality that left no room for appeal.
There was a sense of finality when she closed the door for the last time.

meanings.definition: So slight or gradual that it cannot be noticed or perceived.
examples: The change in temperature was almost imperceptible.
She gave an imperceptible nod that only her partner caught.

meanings.definition: The place where someone or something is located.
examples: The police are trying to determine the suspect's whereabouts.
No one knew the whereabouts of the missing documents.

meanings.definition: A room in a home set apart for receiving and entertaining guests.
examples: They sat in the parlor sipping tea while waiting for dinner.
The old house had a formal parlor with velvet chairs.

meanings.definition: A business establishment providing a particular service, such as a beauty parlor or ice cream parlor.
examples: She booked an appointment at the nail parlor downtown.
We stopped at an ice cream parlor on the way home.

meanings.definition: An elected member of a city or municipal council.
examples: The alderman voted in favor of the new park proposal.
Residents petitioned their alderman to fix the broken streetlights.

meanings.definition: Aware of or having knowledge of something.
examples: The manager was fully cognizant of the risks involved.
She remained cognizant of the time as the meeting dragged on.

meanings.definition: The belief that no God or gods exist.
examples: He embraced atheism after years of questioning his faith.
The book defends atheism as a rational worldview.

meanings.definition: Lacking energy, effort, or enthusiasm; carelessly lazy.
examples: His lackadaisical attitude toward homework worried his teachers.
The team played in a lackadaisical manner and lost the game.

meanings.definition: To secretly cooperate or plot with others to do something wrong.
examples: The two officials connived to embezzle the company's funds.
She suspected they were conniving behind her back.

meanings.definition: To deliberately ignore or pretend not to notice a wrongdoing.
examples: The guard connived at the smuggling in exchange for a bribe.

meanings.definition: A member of a Christian church that broke away from the Roman Catholic Church during the Reformation.
examples: As a Protestant, she attended a Lutheran congregation.
The region was divided between Catholics and Protestants.

meanings.definition: Having a resemblance to something or someone without being identical.
examples: The two paintings use similar colors but different subjects.
My sister and I have similar tastes in music.

meanings.definition: The collected myths, legends, and stories of a particular culture concerning its gods and heroes.
examples: Greek mythology is full of gods, monsters, and epic quests.
She wrote her thesis on the mythology of ancient Egypt.

meanings.definition: A set of widely held but exaggerated or false beliefs about something.
examples: A whole mythology has grown up around the company's founder.

meanings.definition: To collect and study plants, especially in their natural surroundings.
examples: They spent the summer botanizing in the alpine meadows.
The naturalist botanized along the riverbank, pressing each specimen.

meanings.definition: Relating to or offered as a sacrifice.
examples: The ancient altar was used for sacrificial offerings.
Her sacrificial devotion to her family left little time for herself.

meanings.definition: A person who entrusts goods to another for sale, transport, or safekeeping.
examples: The consignor shipped the antiques to the auction house.
As consignor, she received a percentage of each item sold.

meanings.definition: To rise above or go beyond the usual limits of something.
examples: Great art can transcend cultural and language barriers.
Her talent transcends anything I have seen before.

meanings.definition: The outline or contour of something, especially a side view of a face.
examples: The coin showed the queen's profile in sharp relief.
From the side, you could see the elegant profile of the building.

meanings.definition: A short description giving the most important facts about a person or thing.
examples: The magazine published a profile of the rising young author.
She updated her profile on the networking site.

meanings.definition: To shock or stir people into sudden, energetic action.
examples: The speech galvanized the crowd into marching on city hall.
News of the disaster galvanized volunteers across the country.

meanings.definition: To coat iron or steel with a protective layer of zinc.
examples: The fence was galvanized to prevent it from rusting.

meanings.definition: To make something clear by explaining it more fully.
examples: The professor elucidated the difficult theory with a simple diagram.
Could you elucidate what you meant by that remark?

meanings.definition: A right, privilege, or possession that a person is entitled to by birth.
examples: Freedom was considered their birthright as citizens.
He felt the family business was his birthright.

meanings.definition: Sincere regret for wrongdoing along with a desire to make amends.
examples: His repentance seemed genuine as he apologized to those he had hurt.
The prisoner showed real repentance for his crime.

meanings.definition: The quality of being genuine, real, or true to its claimed origin.
examples: Experts confirmed the authenticity of the ancient manuscript.
The restaurant is praised for the authenticity of its recipes.

meanings.definition: A paste or powder used for cleaning the teeth.
examples: Toothpaste is the most common dentifrice today.
The pharmacy sold an herbal dentifrice flavored with mint.

meanings.definition: The act of revealing or making known something secret or private.
examples: The divulgence of the company's plans angered the board.
Any divulgence of the password is strictly forbidden.

meanings.definition: Favoring traditional ways and resistant to sudden change.
examples: Her conservative approach to investing avoided risky stocks.
The town held conservative views on most social issues.

meanings.definition: Deliberately moderate or cautious so as not to overstate.
examples: A conservative estimate puts the cost at one million dollars.

meanings.definition: Agreement or shared opinion on a matter.
examples: The plan went ahead with the concurrence of all members.
There was general concurrence that the policy needed reform.

meanings.definition: Relating to or located in the abdomen.
examples: The patient complained of sharp abdominal pain.
Doctors performed an ultrasound of the abdominal region.

meanings.definition: One of the muscles of the abdomen.
examples: The workout targets the abdominals and lower back.

meanings.definition: To cause someone to feel isolated or to withdraw their affection or support.
examples: His harsh comments alienated many of his closest friends.
The new policy risks alienating loyal customers.

meanings.definition: The purest, most perfect, or most essential example of something.
examples: The little cottage was the quintessence of rural charm.
Her speech captured the quintessence of the movement's ideals.

meanings.definition: A substance, especially an alcoholic drink or drug, that causes intoxication.
examples: Alcohol is the most widely used intoxicant in the world.
The law prohibits the sale of intoxicants to minors.

meanings.definition: The height of something above sea level or above the ground.
examples: The plane cruised at an altitude of thirty thousand feet.
At high altitude, the air becomes thin and cold.

meanings.definition: The act of sending or passing something from one place or person to another.
examples: The transmission of the signal was delayed by the storm.
Vaccines help prevent the transmission of disease.

meanings.definition: The mechanism in a vehicle that transfers power from the engine to the wheels.
examples: The mechanic said the car's transmission needed to be replaced.

meanings.definition: A person whose job is to cut glass and fit it into windows and frames.
examples: The glazier replaced the cracked pane in the front door.
We hired a glazier to install the new storefront windows.

meanings.definition: The act of stopping or interrupting something that was ongoing.
examples: The discontinuance of the bus route upset many commuters.
Patients were warned about the discontinuance of the medication.

meanings.definition: To make someone feel anxious, uneasy, or disturbed.
examples: The strange noise in the attic unsettled the children.
Rumors of layoffs unsettled the entire office.

meanings.definition: The study of God, religious belief, and the nature of the divine.
examples: She studied theology at a seminary before becoming a minister.
Their debate centered on a fine point of medieval theology.

meanings.definition: An upward throw or movement, or a shaft through which air rises in a mine.
examples: Fresh air entered through the upcast of the ventilation system.
The upcast of dust marked where the explosion had occurred.

meanings.definition: To arrange or organize things into groups based on shared characteristics.
examples: Scientists classify living things into kingdoms and species.
The librarian classified the new books by subject.

meanings.definition: To designate information as officially secret and restricted.
examples: The government chose to classify the documents for national security.

meanings.definition: To disturb the order or arrangement of something.
examples: The wind disarranged her carefully combed hair.
Someone had disarranged the papers on his desk.

meanings.definition: Active at night or relating to the night.
examples: Owls are nocturnal hunters that sleep during the day.
She enjoyed the city's nocturnal quiet after midnight.

meanings.definition: A young bull or a castrated bull, often used as a work animal.
examples: The farmer hitched the bullock to the plow.
A pair of bullocks pulled the heavy cart up the hill.

meanings.definition: A change or modification made to something.
examples: The tailor made a small alteration to the hem of the dress.
Several alterations to the original plan were approved.

meanings.definition: The meat of a deer, used as food.
examples: They roasted venison over the open fire.
The restaurant served venison with a rich berry sauce.

meanings.definition: A place where animal hides are made into leather.
examples: The tannery on the river's edge processed hundreds of hides a week.
A strong odor drifted from the old tannery.

meanings.definition: To send or pass something from one place, person, or thing to another.
examples: The radio station transmits its signal across three states.
Mosquitoes can transmit serious diseases to humans.

meanings.definition: With the head leading the way, or in a reckless and hasty manner.
examples: He dived headfirst into the cool water.
She plunged headfirst into the new business without a plan.

meanings.definition: Incapable of being turned aside or diverted.
examples: His indivertible focus on the goal carried him through every setback.
The river followed an indivertible course to the sea.

meanings.definition: Stubborn refusal to obey or respect rightful authority.
examples: The witness was jailed for contumacy after defying the court.
His contumacy toward his superiors led to his dismissal.

meanings.definition: A base or support on which a column, statue, or vase stands.
examples: The marble statue rested on a tall stone pedestal.
An ornate pedestal held the antique vase.

meanings.definition: Playful and humorous; said or done in jest.
examples: His jocose remarks kept the whole table laughing.
She gave a jocose reply to the serious question.

meanings.definition: The longest side of a right triangle, opposite the right angle.
examples: Use the Pythagorean theorem to find the length of the hypotenuse.
The hypotenuse stretched diagonally across the triangle.

meanings.definition: Extremely small in size or amount.
examples: A minute speck of dust floated in the sunlight.
Only minute traces of the chemical were found.

meanings.definition: Marked by close attention to the smallest details.
examples: The inspector conducted a minute examination of the engine.

meanings.definition: Relating to ships, sailors, or navigation at sea.
examples: The museum displayed many nautical instruments from old ships.
He decorated the room with a nautical theme of anchors and ropes.

meanings.definition: Able to be understood.
examples: The instructions were clear and easily comprehensible.
She rewrote the report to make it comprehensible to beginners.

meanings.definition: A metal copy of a printing surface or object made by electroplating.
examples: The printer used an electrotype to reproduce the illustration.
Each coin design began as a carefully crafted electrotype.

meanings.definition: Wandering or traveling in search of adventure.
examples: The errant knight roamed the land righting wrongs.
An errant breeze carried the scent of the sea.

meanings.definition: Straying from the proper course or accepted standards.
examples: An errant golf ball shattered the window.

meanings.definition: Having a keen ability to understand and notice things quickly.
examples: Her perspicacious analysis exposed the flaw everyone else had missed.
A perspicacious reader will catch the hidden clues early on.

meanings.definition: Containing water, especially as part of a chemical compound.
examples: The mineral exists in a hydrous form that holds water molecules.
Hydrous solutions were used throughout the experiment.

meanings.definition: Certain to happen and impossible to avoid.
examples: With both teams exhausted, a draw seemed inevitable.
Change is an inevitable part of life.

meanings.definition: Rude, disrespectful, and arrogant behavior toward others.
examples: The student was punished for his insolence toward the teacher.
She was shocked by the insolence of his reply.

meanings.definition: The combination of qualities and traits that form a person's distinctive character.
examples: Her warm personality made everyone feel welcome.
The twins look alike but have very different personalities.

meanings.definition: A famous or well-known person, especially in entertainment or media.
examples: A popular television personality hosted the charity event.

meanings.definition: Basic or undeveloped; relating to an early or incomplete stage.
examples: He had only a rudimentary knowledge of Spanish.
The first computers were rudimentary compared to today's machines.

meanings.definition: To argue angrily or heatedly in words.
examples: The neighbors altercated loudly over the property line.
They began to altercate the moment the subject of money came up.

meanings.definition: A sharp, harsh taste like that of unsweetened coffee.
examples: The bitterness of the dark chocolate surprised her.
A hint of bitterness lingered in the herbal tea.

meanings.definition: A feeling of deep resentment or anger.
examples: Years of disappointment had filled him with bitterness.

meanings.definition: A swollen, infected area of tissue containing a collection of pus.
examples: The dentist drained the abscess at the root of the tooth.
An abscess formed where the wound had become infected.

meanings.definition: To become involved in a situation in order to change or influence its outcome.
examples: A teacher had to intervene to stop the argument.
The government intervened to stabilize the failing bank.

meanings.definition: To occur or come between two events or points in time.
examples: Several years intervened before they met again.

meanings.definition: Awkward and lacking grace or coordination in movement.
examples: He was so clumsy that he knocked over the vase.
Her clumsy attempt to dance made everyone smile.

meanings.definition: Done without skill or tact; poorly handled.
examples: His clumsy apology only made the situation worse.

meanings.definition: Only partly conscious or aware of one's surroundings.
examples: The injured driver was semiconscious when help arrived.
She lay semiconscious for several minutes after the fall.

meanings.definition: To refuse to accept or have anything to do with someone or something.
examples: She repudiated the accusation as a complete fabrication.
The committee repudiated any connection to the disgraced official.

meanings.definition: To reject the validity or authority of something, such as a debt or agreement.
examples: The new government threatened to repudiate the debts of its predecessor.

meanings.definition: Showing great courage, especially in battle or in the face of danger.
examples: The valorous knight charged into the fray without hesitation.
Her valorous efforts to save the trapped miners earned her a medal.

meanings.definition: Food or anything that nourishes and sustains.
examples: Fresh milk was the chief aliment of the growing child.
The roots drew aliment from the rich, dark soil.

meanings.definition: A historian who records events year by year in chronological order.
examples: The medieval annalist carefully noted each year's harvests and wars.
As an annalist, he documented the kingdom's affairs reign by reign.

meanings.definition: Impossible to overcome or get past.
examples: The lack of funding proved an insuperable obstacle to the project.
They faced insuperable odds, yet refused to surrender.

meanings.definition: Existing or available in large quantities; abundant.
examples: She took copious notes during the lecture.
The garden produced a copious supply of tomatoes that summer.

meanings.definition: A remark or words inserted into a conversation or text, often interrupting it.
examples: His sudden interpolation broke the speaker's train of thought.
Scholars suspect that line is a later interpolation added to the original manuscript.

meanings.definition: In mathematics, the estimation of an unknown value between two known values.
examples: Linear interpolation gave us an approximate reading between the two data points.

meanings.definition: To make something stale or trite through overuse or repetition.
examples: Years of advertising have hackneyed the once-striking phrase.
Don't hackney that joke by telling it at every party.

meanings.definition: To divide something into three parts, especially three equal parts.
examples: The geometry problem asked them to trisect the angle using only a compass.
A wide road trisects the old estate into three uneven fields.

meanings.definition: Devoted to or fond of music, especially orchestral music.
examples: The philharmonic society hosted concerts every winter.
She joined a philharmonic choir to indulge her love of singing.

meanings.definition: The manner or place in which a thing is set or arranged.
examples: He adjusted the lamp's position so the light fell on his book.
From this position on the hill, you can see the whole valley.

meanings.definition: A person's opinion or stance on an issue.
examples: The senator clarified her position on the new tax bill.

meanings.definition: A job or post.
examples: She applied for a position in the marketing department.

meanings.definition: To delay or avoid making a decision in order to gain time.
examples: Rather than answer, the minister temporized with vague promises.
The general temporized, hoping reinforcements would arrive.

meanings.definition: Kind and generous, sincerely wishing for the well-being of others.
examples: The benevolent old man funded scholarships for poor students.
She gave him a benevolent smile and offered to help.

meanings.definition: The state of being cheerful and lighthearted; merriment.
examples: The festival filled the streets with music and gaiety.
Her infectious gaiety lifted everyone's spirits.

meanings.definition: Writing or handwriting in the ordinary cursive form.
examples: The letter was penned in an elegant, flowing script.
She learned to read the old Latin script of the manuscript.

meanings.definition: The written text of a play, film, or broadcast.
examples: The actor memorized his lines from the script overnight.

meanings.definition: A large fleet of warships.
examples: The Spanish armada set sail to invade England.
An armada of fishing boats crowded the harbor at dawn.

meanings.definition: The level of confidence, enthusiasm, and morale of a person or group.
examples: The team's morale soared after their unexpected victory.
Long hours and low pay had crushed the workers' morale.

meanings.definition: Too severe, painful, or unpleasant to be endured.
examples: The heat in the unventilated room was intolerable.
His constant rudeness became intolerable to his coworkers.

meanings.definition: Work done for the benefit or assistance of another.
examples: She devoted her life to the service of the poor.
His years of loyal service to the company were recognized at the dinner.

meanings.definition: A system supplying a public need such as transport or utilities.
examples: The bus service runs every fifteen minutes.

meanings.definition: A union of states or people joined together for mutual support or a common purpose.
examples: The tribes formed a confederacy to resist the invaders.
A loose confederacy of merchants controlled the trade routes.

meanings.definition: A machine for hoisting and moving heavy weights, with a movable boom.
examples: A towering derrick lowered the steel beams into place.
The oil derrick rose high above the flat plain.

meanings.definition: Deserving of harsh criticism or blame.
examples: His vituperable conduct during the trial shocked the jury.
Such a vituperable betrayal earned him universal scorn.

meanings.definition: To feel or show aversion, antagonism, or dislike.
examples: He antipathized toward anyone who flattered him too openly.
The two rivals antipathized from their very first meeting.

meanings.definition: The skill or quality of work shown in something made by hand.
examples: The fine workmanship of the carved chest amazed the buyers.
Poor workmanship caused the bridge to fail within a year.

meanings.definition: The act of moving or going forward.
examples: The slow precession of the seasons marked the passing year.
A steady precession of mourners filed past the grave.

meanings.definition: In physics, the slow circular movement of the axis of a spinning object.
examples: The precession of Earth's axis causes the pole star to change over millennia.

meanings.definition: A turning away from the true meaning, proper purpose, or normal course of something.
examples: The dictator's rule was a perversion of every democratic ideal.
Critics called the policy a perversion of justice.

meanings.definition: An act of retaliation, especially against an enemy in response to an injury.
examples: The raid was carried out in reprisal for the earlier attack.
Workers feared reprisals if they spoke out against management.

meanings.definition: Joined together, especially in pairs.
examples: The two conjugate angles together formed a full rotation.
In chemistry, an acid and its conjugate base differ by a single proton.

meanings.definition: A short biographical sketch or notice of a person's life.
examples: The anthology included a brief biograph of each contributing author.
A modest biograph appeared beneath the scholar's portrait.

meanings.definition: The state of being varied or different; variety.
examples: The region is known for its great diversity of plant life.
A diversity of opinions enriched the committee's debate.

meanings.definition: A state of extreme poverty or need.
examples: After losing his job, the family sank into indigence.
Charities were set up to relieve the indigence of the city's poor.

meanings.definition: To catch sight of or make out something, especially with effort.
examples: From the tower, the sentry could just descry ships on the horizon.
She descried a faint light flickering in the distant valley.

meanings.definition: A person skilled in or who studies metaphysics, the philosophy of being and reality.
examples: The metaphysician argued that time itself might be an illusion.
As a metaphysician, she pondered the nature of existence.

meanings.definition: Failing to plan or provide for the future; not thrifty.
examples: His improvident spending left him penniless by middle age.
The improvident farmer ate his seed grain and had nothing to plant.

meanings.definition: Steady, continued adherence to a purpose, course, or task despite difficulty.
examples: Her persistence finally wore down the committee's resistance.
Through sheer persistence, he taught himself three languages.

meanings.definition: To declare or greet with loud approval and a shout.
examples: The crowd acclaimed the runner as she crossed the finish line.
They acclaimed him champion before the cheering stadium.

meanings.definition: To praise someone or something publicly and enthusiastically.
examples: Critics acclaimed her novel as a masterpiece.

meanings.definition: Lasting for a very short time.
examples: There was a momentary pause before the applause erupted.
A momentary lapse in attention caused the accident.

meanings.definition: A union or combination of separate parts or groups into a single body.
examples: Several small parties formed a coalition to win the election.
The coalition of charities pooled their resources for the relief effort.

meanings.definition: Able to be put or to operate underwater.
examples: The submersible pump can run for hours beneath the surface.
Engineers tested the submersible vessel in the deep tank.

meanings.definition: A substance that destroys or prevents the growth of harmful microorganisms.
examples: The nurse cleaned the wound with an antiseptic before bandaging it.
He kept an antiseptic in the first-aid kit for cuts and scrapes.

meanings.definition: The intervening time between two events or periods.
examples: The new manager starts next month; in the interim, the deputy will lead.
Much had changed in the interim since they last met.

meanings.definition: Information, often biased or misleading, spread systematically to promote a cause or viewpoint.
examples: Wartime propaganda urged citizens to ration food.
The pamphlet was dismissed as mere propaganda.

meanings.definition: A means of transport; a vehicle.
examples: A horse-drawn cart was their only conveyance to town.
The hotel provided conveyance from the airport.

meanings.definition: The act of transferring property or rights from one person to another.
examples: The lawyer handled the conveyance of the house to its new owners.

meanings.definition: Round and plump in shape.
examples: The rotund chef beamed as he carried out the feast.
A rotund little teapot sat on the shelf.

meanings.definition: Full and rich in sound or style.
examples: The speaker delivered the address in rotund, sonorous tones.

meanings.definition: Awareness or knowledge of something gained through the senses.
examples: Bright light can distort our perception of distance.
His keen perception caught details that others missed.

meanings.definition: The way in which something is understood or interpreted.
examples: Public perception of the company improved after the apology.

meanings.definition: The act of planning, devising, or inventing something for a particular purpose.
examples: Their escape was the result of careful contrivance.
By clever contrivance, she solved the puzzle no one else could.

meanings.definition: A device or gadget, especially one ingeniously made.
examples: He rigged up an odd contrivance of ropes and pulleys to lift the load.

meanings.definition: Able to speak two languages fluently.
examples: Growing up in Montreal, she became bilingual in English and French.
The company hired a bilingual receptionist to serve more customers.

meanings.definition: The state of keeping something hidden or concealed from others.
examples: The plans were drawn up in complete secrecy.
Sworn to secrecy, the guards revealed nothing.

meanings.definition: To protect something from harm, loss, or danger.
examples: New laws were passed to safeguard workers' rights.
She kept the documents in a vault to safeguard them against fire.

meanings.definition: To pay off or settle a debt by delivering its full amount or value.
examples: He worked two jobs to liquidate the loan he had taken.
The company sold assets to liquidate its outstanding obligations.

meanings.definition: To close down a business and convert its assets into cash.
examples: When the firm went bankrupt, the court ordered it to liquidate.

meanings.definition: Laying down or setting aside.
examples: In Latin grammar, a deponent verb is passive in form but active in meaning.
The deponent clause set aside the earlier terms of the contract.

meanings.definition: Unable to offer resistance; powerless.
examples: Resistless before the storm, the small boat was tossed about.
The crowd stood resistless as the soldiers advanced.

meanings.definition: Impossible to resist; irresistible.
examples: The flood swept on with resistless force.

meanings.definition: A person who rules a colony or province on behalf of a sovereign, with royal authority.
examples: The viceroy governed the distant colony in the king's name.
As viceroy, she answered only to the crown across the sea.

meanings.definition: A writer of inferior or worthless poetry.
examples: The critics dismissed him as a mere poetaster.
Every age produces its poetasters alongside its true poets.

meanings.definition: Self-righteously concerned with propriety; smugly conceited about one's own correctness.
examples: His priggish disapproval of the slightest slang annoyed everyone.
She found his priggish manners insufferable at parties.

meanings.definition: Full of juice; tender and moist.
examples: They feasted on succulent slices of roast lamb.
The peach was ripe and succulent.

meanings.definition: Describing a plant with thick, fleshy parts that store water.
examples: Cacti and other succulent plants thrive in dry climates.

meanings.definition: Clearly defined, exact, and free from doubt.
examples: We need a definite answer by Friday.
She had a definite idea of how the room should look.

meanings.definition: To gradually fail, diminish, or come to an end (usually used with 'out').
examples: Their enthusiasm petered out as the project dragged on.
The trail petered out into thick underbrush.

meanings.definition: A quiet, secret, or concealed manner of acting to avoid being noticed.
examples: The cat approached the bird with practiced stealth.
By stealth, the spy slipped past the guards unseen.

meanings.definition: Unwillingness or inability to accept views, beliefs, or people different from one's own.
examples: The campaign fought against religious intolerance.
His intolerance of any criticism made him hard to work with.

meanings.definition: A physical inability to tolerate a particular food or substance.
examples: Her lactose intolerance means she avoids dairy products.
